category_title: Kindergarten students with ASD who use classroom scripts and task-avoidance to seek social interaction
minimal_context: Jasmine is sitting at a round table with a peer and a paraprofessional, transitioning to a math worksheet where she needs to color objects in groups of 4.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Jasmine, it is time to color the objects on the page in groups of 4 on your math worksheet.

STUDENT: (Looks at Jeff) Let’s do some colors! What color is the apple? What color is the boy? What color is the car? (Excitedly shakes her braids and rubs her fingers together quickly in front of the ABC book)

TUTOR: Please look at your own paper and keep working.

STUDENT: (Leans over the table reaching toward Jeff and, waving her arms) booga booga.

TUTOR: (Points to the math worksheet) Jasmine, we have music next. You need to do your math.

STUDENT: (Grabs the ABC book again) No! Aaahh!

options.A: Stop Jasmine's behavior, then give an explanation that not all the objects on the math worksheet are colored yet and she needs to finish the activity.
options.B: Teach Jasmine the technique of 'stepping back' and not acting on her 'first thought' when transitioning to the math worksheet and interacting with her peers.
options.C: Ask Jasmine higher-order questions about the math worksheet, provide a more challenging alternative activity, or turn the coloring task into an opportunity for social interaction with Jeff.
options.D: Allow Jasmine to work on the math worksheet at her own pace and take a break from coloring the objects as and when she wants during the session.
